title: The Complete Guide to ETF Investing | Westmount Fundamentals
description: Most investors pick ETFs by name recognition alone. Learn the metrics that actually matter — expense ratios, tracking error, tax efficiency — and build a smarter portfolio.

schemas.headline: The Complete Guide to ETF Investing
schemas.description: A comprehensive educational guide on ETF investing. Learn what ETFs are, types, key metrics like expense ratios, tax efficiency, and how to build a portfolio.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between an ETF and a mutual fund?

acceptedAnswer.text: ETFs trade on stock exchanges throughout the day at market-determined prices, while mutual funds are priced only once per day at their Net Asset Value (NAV). ETFs generally have lower expense ratios and are more tax-efficient than mutual funds.

Yes, ETFs are excellent for beginners. They provide instant diversification, low costs, and ease of trading. Broad-market index ETFs allow investors to own a small piece of thousands of companies with a single purchase.
Yes, if the underlying stocks or bonds in the ETF pay dividends or interest, the ETF will collect those payments and distribute them to shareholders, typically on a quarterly basis.
